questions.question: Which class of airspace typically surrounds the busiest airports (e.g., major international airports) and extends from the surface to 10,000 feet MSL?
questions.explanation: Class B airspace surrounds the nation's busiest airports, from the surface to typically 10,000 feet MSL. It requires ATC clearance to enter and a Mode C transponder.

questions.question: What altitude range defines Class A airspace in the contiguous United States?
questions.explanation: Class A airspace extends from 18,000 feet MSL to FL600. All operations must be under IFR, and pilots must have an instrument rating.

questions.question: What is required to enter Class C airspace?
questions.correct_answer: Two-way radio communication with ATC and a Mode C transponder
distractors: An ATC clearance, like Class B
Only a transponder with Mode C
No requirements beyond those for Class E
questions.explanation: To enter Class C, you must establish two-way radio communication with approach control (ATC must acknowledge your callsign) and have a Mode C transponder.

questions.question: What happens to Class D airspace when the control tower closes?
questions.correct_answer: It typically reverts to Class E or Class G airspace
distractors: It remains Class D with CTAF procedures
questions.explanation: When the tower closes, Class D airspace typically reverts to Class E (if shown on charts) or Class G. Pilots should use CTAF procedures.

questions.question: In most of the contiguous US, at what altitude does Class E airspace begin if no other airspace is designated?
questions.explanation: Unless otherwise designated, Class E airspace begins at 1,200 feet AGL in most areas. It can extend from 700 feet AGL near airports with instrument approaches, or from the surface in some cases.

questions.question: What are the VFR visibility and cloud clearance requirements in Class G airspace below 1,200 feet AGL during the day?
questions.correct_answer: 1 statute mile visibility, clear of clouds
distractors: 3 statute miles visibility, 500 below/1000 above/2000 horizontal from clouds
5 statute miles visibility, clear of clouds
1 statute mile visibility, 500 below/1000 above/2000 horizontal
questions.explanation: In Class G below 1,200 feet AGL during the day, the minimums are 1 SM visibility and clear of clouds. At night, it's 3 SM and 500/1000/2000.

questions.question: What type of special use airspace contains hazardous activities such as artillery firing or aerial gunnery, and requires authorization to fly through when active?
questions.explanation: Restricted areas contain hazardous activities (artillery, gunnery, missile testing). Non-participating aircraft need authorization from the controlling agency to enter when active. A Prohibited area forbids all flight at all times. MOAs and Alert areas allow VFR traffic without authorization.

questions.question: What is a TFR?
questions.correct_answer: A Temporary Flight Restriction, a short-term airspace restriction for safety or security
distractors: A Transponder Frequency Requirement for Class B
A Terminal Flight Route for arriving IFR traffic
A Transition Flight Rule for changing airspace classes
questions.explanation: TFRs temporarily restrict aircraft from specific areas due to hazards, VIP movement, disaster relief, space operations, or major sporting events. They are published as NOTAMs.
